title: Customers With No Orders
statement: Find the customer_id of customers who never placed an order.
hint: Use a LEFT JOIN from customers to orders, then filter rows with no matching order.
explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps all customers. Customers without a matching order will have NULL values on the orders side.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT c.customer_id FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id WHERE o.order_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER, customer_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT);

title: Top 3 Products by Revenue
statement: Find the top 3 products by total revenue. Output product_name and revenue, ordered by revenue descending.
hint: Join products and sales, aggregate quantity * price, then sort and limit.
explanation: Revenue is quantity multiplied by price. After joining the two tables, group by product and sum revenue.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT p.product_name, SUM(s.quantity * s.price) AS revenue FROM products p JOIN sales s ON p.product_id = s.product_id GROUP BY p.product_name ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 3
schema: CREATE TABLE products (product_id INTEGER, product_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, product_id INTEGER, quantity INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: Average Order Value
statement: Find the average order value from the orders table.
hint: Use AVG on total_amount.
explanation: AVG computes the mean across all rows in total_amount.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT ROUND(AVG(total_amount), 2) AS avg_order_value FROM orders
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, total_amount REAL);

title: Monthly Active Users
statement: Count unique users per month in 2024. Output month and active_users ordered by month.
hint: Group by the first day of each month and count distinct user_id.
explanation: In SQLite, use date(created_at, 'start of month') to normalize each event date to the first day of its month, then group and count distinct users.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT date(created_at, 'start of month') AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS active_users FROM events WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01' GROUP BY date(created_at, 'start of month') ORDER BY month
schema: CREATE TABLE events (event_id INTEGER, user_id INTEGER, created_at TEXT);

title: Customers With More Than 2 Orders
statement: Find customer_id values for customers who placed more than 2 orders.
hint: Group by customer_id and use HAVING.
explanation: COUNT gives orders per customer, and HAVING filters aggregated groups.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT customer_id FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 2
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER);

title: Latest Order Per Customer
statement: For each customer, return their latest order_date.
hint: Group by customer_id and use MAX(order_date).
explanation: MAX on order_date finds the most recent order in each customer group.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) AS latest_order_date FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT);

title: Second Highest Salary
statement: Find the second highest distinct salary from the employees table.
hint: Use MAX in a subquery to exclude the highest salary first.
explanation: First find the maximum salary, then find the maximum salary smaller than that value.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest_salary FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, employee_name TEXT, salary INTEGER);

title: Employees Above Department Average
statement: Find employee_name values for employees whose salary is greater than the average salary of their department.
hint: Use a correlated subquery on department.
explanation: A correlated subquery can compute the average salary within the current employee's department and compare each employee against it.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT e1.employee_name FROM employees e1 WHERE e1.salary > (SELECT AVG(e2.salary) FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.department = e1.department)
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, employee_name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INTEGER);

title: Running Total of Daily Sales
statement: For each sale_date, return sale_date, amount, and running_total ordered by sale_date.
hint: Use SUM(amount) OVER with ORDER BY sale_date.
explanation: A window function can accumulate values row by row without collapsing the result into grouped rows.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT sale_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS running_total FROM sales ORDER BY sale_date
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, sale_date TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Top Seller Per Category
statement: Return the top-selling product_name in each category based on total_units.
hint: Use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by category ordered by total_units descending.
explanation: ROW_NUMBER can rank products within each category, and then you can keep only rank 1.
acceptedAnswers: WITH ranked AS (SELECT category, product_name, total_units, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY total_units DESC) AS rn FROM product_sales) SELECT category, product_name, total_units FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE product_sales (product_id INTEGER, product_name TEXT, category TEXT, total_units INTEGER);

title: Orders Without Delivery
statement: Find order_id values for orders that do not have a matching delivery record.
hint: Use a LEFT JOIN from orders to deliveries and filter NULLs on the deliveries side.
explanation: A LEFT JOIN preserves all orders. Orders without a matching delivery will have NULL values in the delivery columns.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT o.order_id FROM orders o LEFT JOIN deliveries d ON o.order_id = d.order_id WHERE d.delivery_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE deliveries (delivery_id INTEGER, order_id INTEGER, delivered_at TEXT);

title: Users With 3 Or More Sessions
statement: Find user_id values for users with at least 3 sessions.
hint: Group by user_id and use HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3.
explanation: COUNT aggregates the number of sessions per user, and HAVING filters the grouped results.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT user_id FROM sessions GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
schema: CREATE TABLE sessions (session_id INTEGER, user_id INTEGER, started_at TEXT);

title: First Order Date Per Customer
statement: For each customer, return customer_id and first_order_date.
hint: Group by customer_id and use MIN(order_date).
explanation: MIN on order_date gives the earliest order for each customer.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT);

title: Customers Who Ordered Every Month
statement: Find customer_id values for customers who placed at least one order in every month present in the dataset.
hint: Count distinct months per customer and compare it to the total distinct months in the table.
explanation: A subquery can compute the number of distinct months overall, while the outer query checks which customers match that count.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT customer_id FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT strftime('%Y-%m', order_date)) = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT strftime('%Y-%m', order_date)) FROM orders)
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT);

title: Products Never Sold
statement: Find the product_name of products that have never appeared in the sales table.
hint: Use a LEFT JOIN from products to sales, then keep rows with no matching sale.
explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps all products. Unsold products will have NULL values from the sales table.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT p.product_name FROM products p LEFT JOIN sales s ON p.product_id = s.product_id WHERE s.sale_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE products (product_id INTEGER, product_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, product_id INTEGER, quantity INTEGER);

title: Orders With Customer Names
statement: Return order_id and customer_name for each order.
hint: Join orders to customers using customer_id.
explanation: An INNER JOIN combines order rows with the matching customer row.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT o.order_id, c.customer_name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER, customer_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER);

title: Employees and Their Managers
statement: Return employee_name and manager_name for employees who have a manager.
hint: Use a self join on employees where manager_id matches employee_id.
explanation: A self join lets one copy of the employees table represent workers and the other represent their managers.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT e.employee_name, m.employee_name AS manager_name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.employee_id
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, employee_name TEXT, manager_id INTEGER);

title: Customers With Total Spend Over 100
statement: Find customer_name and total_spend for customers whose total spend is greater than 100.
hint: Join customers and orders, group by customer, sum amount, then filter with HAVING.
explanation: After joining orders to customers, aggregate order amounts per customer and use HAVING to keep totals above 100.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT c.customer_name, SUM(o.amount) AS total_spend FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name HAVING SUM(o.amount) > 100
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER, customer_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Daily Revenue
statement: For each sale_date, return sale_date and revenue ordered by sale_date.
hint: Group by sale_date and sum amount.
explanation: SUM adds all sale amounts for the same date.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT sale_date, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date ORDER BY sale_date
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, sale_date TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Distinct Products Sold Per Day
statement: For each sale_date, return the number of distinct products sold as distinct_products ordered by sale_date.
hint: Use COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) grouped by sale_date.
explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) counts unique products sold each day.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT sale_date, COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) AS distinct_products FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date ORDER BY sale_date
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, sale_date TEXT, product_id INTEGER);

title: Average Rating Per Product
statement: Return product_id and avg_rating rounded to 2 decimals, ordered by product_id.
hint: Group by product_id and use ROUND(AVG(rating), 2).
explanation: AVG computes each product's mean rating, and ROUND formats the result consistently.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT product_id, ROUND(AVG(rating), 2) AS avg_rating FROM reviews GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY product_id
schema: CREATE TABLE reviews (review_id INTEGER, product_id INTEGER, rating REAL);

title: High Value Customers By Average Spend
statement: Find customer_id values for customers whose average order amount is greater than 50.
hint: Group by customer_id and use HAVING AVG(amount) > 50.
explanation: AVG calculates the mean order amount for each customer and HAVING filters those grouped results.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT customer_id FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING AVG(amount) > 50
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Monthly Revenue
statement: Return month and revenue for each month in 2024 ordered by month.
hint: Use date(paid_at, 'start of month') and SUM(amount).
explanation: date(..., 'start of month') buckets payments into months, then SUM aggregates monthly revenue.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT date(paid_at, 'start of month') AS month, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM payments WHERE paid_at >= '2024-01-01' AND paid_at < '2025-01-01' GROUP BY date(paid_at, 'start of month') ORDER BY month
schema: CREATE TABLE payments (payment_id INTEGER, paid_at TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Products Above Average Price
statement: Find product_name values for products priced above the overall average price.
hint: Use a subquery that computes AVG(price).
explanation: The subquery returns one value: the average price across all products. The outer query keeps rows above that value.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT product_name FROM products WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products)
schema: CREATE TABLE products (product_id INTEGER, product_name TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Customers Without Orders Using NOT EXISTS
statement: Find the customer_id of customers who never placed an order using NOT EXISTS.
hint: Filter customers where no matching order exists in a correlated subquery.
explanation: NOT EXISTS checks whether the subquery returns any matching rows for each customer.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT c.customer_id FROM customers c WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id)
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER, customer_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER);

title: Employees With Highest Salary
statement: Find employee_name values for employees who earn the maximum salary.
hint: Compare salary to a subquery with MAX(salary).
explanation: The subquery finds the maximum salary and the outer query returns all employees with that amount.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT employee_name FROM employees WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, employee_name TEXT, salary INTEGER);

title: Duplicate Emails
statement: Find email values that appear more than once in the users table.
hint: Group by email and use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1.
explanation: Grouping by email exposes duplicates, and HAVING keeps only repeated values.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT email FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
schema: CREATE TABLE users (user_id INTEGER, email TEXT);

title: Orders Above Customer Average
statement: Return order_id values for orders whose amount is greater than that customer's average order amount.
hint: Use a correlated subquery on customer_id.
explanation: For each order, a correlated subquery computes that customer's average order amount, and the outer query compares the current row to it.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT o1.order_id FROM orders o1 WHERE o1.amount > (SELECT AVG(o2.amount) FROM orders o2 WHERE o2.customer_id = o1.customer_id)
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Salary Rank
statement: Return employee_name, salary, and salary_rank using RANK ordered by salary descending.
hint: Use RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC).
explanation: RANK assigns the same rank to ties and leaves gaps after ties.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT employee_name, salary, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC, employee_name
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, employee_name TEXT, salary INTEGER);

title: Dense Rank Products By Price
statement: Return product_name, price, and price_rank using DENSE_RANK ordered by price descending.
hint: Use DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC).
explanation: DENSE_RANK assigns the same rank to ties without leaving gaps.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT product_name, price, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC) AS price_rank FROM products ORDER BY price DESC, product_name
schema: CREATE TABLE products (product_id INTEGER, product_name TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Previous Day Sales
statement: For each sale_date, return sale_date, amount, and previous_amount using LAG ordered by sale_date.
hint: Use LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date).
explanation: LAG reads the previous row's value without needing a self join.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT sale_date, amount, LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS previous_amount FROM sales ORDER BY sale_date
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, sale_date TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Top 2 Products Per Category
statement: Return category, product_name, and total_units for the top 2 products in each category by total_units.
hint: Use ROW_NUMBER partitioned by category ordered by total_units descending, then keep rows with rn <= 2.
explanation: ROW_NUMBER can rank products inside each category independently.
acceptedAnswers: WITH ranked AS (SELECT category, product_name, total_units, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY total_units DESC, product_name) AS rn FROM product_sales) SELECT category, product_name, total_units FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 2 ORDER BY category, total_units DESC, product_name
schema: CREATE TABLE product_sales (product_id INTEGER, product_name TEXT, category TEXT, total_units INTEGER);

title: First Order Per Customer With CTE
statement: Using a CTE, return customer_id and first_order_date for each customer.
hint: Build a CTE that groups orders by customer_id and computes MIN(order_date).
explanation: A CTE can make an aggregation step easier to read before selecting from it.
acceptedAnswers: WITH first_orders AS (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT customer_id, first_order_date FROM first_orders ORDER BY customer_id
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT);

title: Top Revenue Product Per Category
statement: Return category, product_name, and revenue for the highest revenue product in each category.
hint: Aggregate revenue first, then rank products within each category.
explanation: This combines aggregation with ranking to solve a classic top-per-group problem.
acceptedAnswers: WITH product_revenue AS (SELECT category, product_name, SUM(quantity * price) AS revenue FROM sales GROUP BY category, product_name), ranked AS (SELECT category, product_name, revenue, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY revenue DESC, product_name) AS rn FROM product_revenue) SELECT category, product_name, revenue FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY category
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_id INTEGER, category TEXT, product_name TEXT, quantity INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: High Value Orders Using CTE
statement: Find order_id values where amount is greater than 100 using a CTE.
hint: Create a CTE and filter in outer query.
explanation: CTE allows you to define a temporary result set and reuse it.
acceptedAnswers: WITH high_orders AS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE amount > 100) SELECT order_id FROM high_orders
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Customer Total Spend with CTE
statement: Return customer_id and total spend using a CTE.
hint: Aggregate inside CTE.
explanation: CTE simplifies grouped calculations.
acceptedAnswers: WITH spend AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spend FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT * FROM spend
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (customer_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: High Spending Customers
statement: Find customers with total spend greater than 100.
hint: Use CTE then filter.
explanation: CTE separates aggregation and filtering logic.
acceptedAnswers: WITH totals AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT customer_id FROM totals WHERE total > 100
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (customer_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Join with CTE
statement: Join customer spend with customers table.
hint: Create spend CTE then join.
acceptedAnswers: WITH spend AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT c.customer_id, total FROM customers c JOIN spend s ON c.customer_id = s.customer_id
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (customer_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Multiple CTEs
statement: Use two CTEs to compute total and average.
acceptedAnswers: WITH totals AS (SELECT SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders), avg_cte AS (SELECT AVG(amount) AS avg FROM orders) SELECT total, avg FROM totals, avg_cte
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (amount INTEGER);

title: Running Total Using CTE
statement: Compute running total using a CTE.
acceptedAnswers: WITH base AS (SELECT * FROM sales) SELECT sale_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS running_total FROM base
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_date TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Remove Duplicate Rows
statement: Remove duplicate emails using ROW_NUMBER and CTE.
acceptedAnswers: WITH ranked AS (SELECT email, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY email) AS rn FROM users) SELECT email FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE users (email TEXT);

title: Top Product Per Category
statement: Return top product per category.
acceptedAnswers: WITH ranked AS (SELECT category, product, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) rn FROM products) SELECT category, product FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE products (category TEXT, product TEXT, sales INTEGER);

title: Layered Query with CTE
statement: Compute average and filter above it using a CTE.
acceptedAnswers: WITH avg_cte AS (SELECT AVG(amount) AS avg_val FROM orders) SELECT order_id FROM orders, avg_cte WHERE amount > avg_val
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER);

title: Customers With Multiple Orders
statement: Find customer_name values for customers who have placed more than 1 order.
hint: Join customers and orders, then group by customer.
explanation: After joining customers with orders, grouping allows counting orders per customer and filtering those with more than one.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT c.customer_name FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_name HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) > 1
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER, customer_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER);

title: Orders With Missing Customers
statement: Find order_id values for orders that do not have a matching customer.
hint: LEFT JOIN from orders to customers.
explanation: Using a LEFT JOIN from orders ensures all orders are included. Orders without matching customers will have NULL values on the customer side.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT o.order_id FROM orders o LEFT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE c.customer_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER);

title: Rank Employees by Salary
statement: Assign a rank to employees based on salary descending.
hint: Use RANK() OVER.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT employee_name, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank FROM employees
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (employee_name TEXT, salary INTEGER);

title: Dense Rank Salaries
statement: Assign dense rank based on salary descending.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank FROM employees
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (salary INTEGER);

title: Row Number Per Department
statement: Assign row numbers within each department ordered by salary descending.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT department, employee_name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS row_num FROM employees
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (department TEXT, employee_name TEXT, salary INTEGER);

title: Top Product Per Category
statement: Return top product per category based on sales.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT category, product FROM (SELECT category, product, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) rn FROM products) WHERE rn = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE products (category TEXT, product TEXT, sales INTEGER);

title: Second Highest Salary Using Rank
statement: Find the second highest salary using ranking.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT salary FROM (SELECT salary, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) r FROM employees) WHERE r = 2
schema: CREATE TABLE employees (salary INTEGER);

title: Products Above Average Price
statement: Find products priced above average.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT product_name FROM products WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products)
schema: CREATE TABLE products (product_name TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Customers Without Orders (NOT EXISTS)
statement: Find customers who never placed orders using NOT EXISTS.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT customer_id FROM customers c WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id)
schema: CREATE TABLE customers (customer_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (customer_id INTEGER);

title: Running Total
statement: Compute running total of sales.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT sale_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS running_total FROM sales
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_date TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Previous Day Sales
statement: Return previous day's sales using LAG.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT sale_date, amount, LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS prev_amount FROM sales
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (sale_date TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Active Listings Per City
statement: Return city and number of active listings.
hint: Filter active listings and group by city.
explanation: Count listings where is_active = 1 grouped by city.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS active_listings FROM listings WHERE is_active = 1 GROUP BY city
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (listing_id INTEGER, city TEXT, is_active INTEGER);

title: Total Revenue Per Host
statement: Return host_id and total booking revenue.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT l.host_id, SUM(b.price) AS revenue FROM listings l JOIN bookings b ON l.listing_id = b.listing_id GROUP BY l.host_id
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (listing_id INTEGER, host_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE bookings (booking_id INTEGER, listing_id INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: Hosts With No Bookings
statement: Find host_id values for hosts with no bookings.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT DISTINCT l.host_id FROM listings l LEFT JOIN bookings b ON l.listing_id = b.listing_id WHERE b.booking_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (listing_id INTEGER, host_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE bookings (booking_id INTEGER, listing_id INTEGER);

title: Average Listing Price Per City
statement: Return city and average listing price.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT city, AVG(price) AS avg_price FROM listings GROUP BY city
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (city TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Top Listing Per City
statement: Return the highest priced listing per city.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT city, listing_id FROM (SELECT city, listing_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY price DESC) rn FROM listings) WHERE rn = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (listing_id INTEGER, city TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Monthly Bookings
statement: Return month and number of bookings.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT date(created_at, 'start of month') AS month, COUNT(*) AS bookings FROM bookings GROUP BY month
schema: CREATE TABLE bookings (created_at TEXT);

title: Listings Above City Average
statement: Find listings priced above their city average.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT listing_id FROM listings l1 WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM listings l2 WHERE l1.city = l2.city)
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (listing_id INTEGER, city TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Running Revenue
statement: Compute running total revenue by booking date.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT booking_date, price, SUM(price) OVER (ORDER BY booking_date) AS running_total FROM bookings
schema: CREATE TABLE bookings (booking_date TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Top 2 Hosts by Revenue
statement: Return top 2 hosts by total revenue.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT host_id FROM (SELECT l.host_id, SUM(b.price) AS revenue, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(b.price) DESC) r FROM listings l JOIN bookings b ON l.listing_id = b.listing_id GROUP BY l.host_id) WHERE r <= 2
schema: CREATE TABLE listings (listing_id INTEGER, host_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE bookings (listing_id INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: Total Sales Per Seller
statement: Return seller_id and total sales revenue.
hint: Use SUM(price) and GROUP BY seller_id.
explanation: Aggregate sales per seller to compute total revenue.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT seller_id, SUM(price) AS total_sales FROM orders GROUP BY seller_id
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, seller_id INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: Top Selling Product
statement: Find the product with the highest total sales.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT product_id FROM (SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(quantity) DESC) r FROM sales GROUP BY product_id) WHERE r = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE sales (product_id INTEGER, quantity INTEGER);

title: Buyers With Multiple Purchases
statement: Find buyer_id values who made more than 1 purchase.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT buyer_id FROM orders GROUP BY buyer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (buyer_id INTEGER);

title: Products Never Sold
statement: Find product_id values that were never sold.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT p.product_id FROM products p LEFT JOIN sales s ON p.product_id = s.product_id WHERE s.product_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE products (product_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE sales (product_id INTEGER);

title: Average Order Value Per Buyer
statement: Return buyer_id and average order value.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT buyer_id, AVG(price) AS avg_order_value FROM orders GROUP BY buyer_id
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (buyer_id INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: Sellers Above Average Revenue
statement: Find sellers whose total revenue is above average.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT seller_id FROM (SELECT seller_id, SUM(price) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY seller_id) WHERE total > (SELECT AVG(total) FROM (SELECT SUM(price) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY seller_id))
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (seller_id INTEGER, price INTEGER);

title: Running Sales Per Seller
statement: Compute running total of sales per seller ordered by date.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT seller_id, order_date, price, SUM(price) OVER (PARTITION BY seller_id ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total FROM orders
schema: CREATE TABLE orders (seller_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT, price INTEGER);

title: Total Watch Time Per User
statement: Return user_id and total watch time.
hint: Use SUM(watch_time).
explanation: Aggregate watch_time per user.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_time) AS total_watch_time FROM watch_history GROUP BY user_id
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (user_id INTEGER, watch_time INTEGER);

title: Most Watched Show
statement: Find the show_id with the highest total watch time.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT show_id FROM (SELECT show_id, SUM(watch_time) AS total, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(watch_time) DESC) r FROM watch_history GROUP BY show_id) WHERE r = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (show_id INTEGER, watch_time INTEGER);

title: Users With No Watch Activity
statement: Find user_id values who have never watched any content.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT u.user_id FROM users u LEFT JOIN watch_history w ON u.user_id = w.user_id WHERE w.user_id IS NULL
schema: CREATE TABLE users (user_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE watch_history (user_id INTEGER);

title: Average Watch Time Per Show
statement: Return show_id and average watch time.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT show_id, AVG(watch_time) AS avg_watch_time FROM watch_history GROUP BY show_id
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (show_id INTEGER, watch_time INTEGER);

title: Top Show Per Genre
statement: Return the most watched show per genre.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT genre, show_id FROM (SELECT genre, show_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY genre ORDER BY SUM(watch_time) DESC) rn FROM watch_history GROUP BY genre, show_id) WHERE rn = 1
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (genre TEXT, show_id INTEGER, watch_time INTEGER);

title: Shows Above Average Watch Time
statement: Find shows whose total watch time is above average.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT show_id FROM (SELECT show_id, SUM(watch_time) AS total FROM watch_history GROUP BY show_id) WHERE total > (SELECT AVG(total) FROM (SELECT SUM(watch_time) AS total FROM watch_history GROUP BY show_id))
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (show_id INTEGER, watch_time INTEGER);

title: Running Watch Time
statement: Compute running watch time per user ordered by date.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT user_id, watch_date, watch_time, SUM(watch_time) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY watch_date) AS running_total FROM watch_history
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (user_id INTEGER, watch_date TEXT, watch_time INTEGER);

title: Users With Multiple Watch Days
statement: Find users who watched content on more than 1 distinct day.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT user_id FROM watch_history GROUP BY user_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT watch_date) > 1
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (user_id INTEGER, watch_date TEXT);

title: Top 2 Users by Watch Time
statement: Return top 2 users by total watch time.
acceptedAnswers: SELECT user_id FROM (SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_time) AS total, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(watch_time) DESC) r FROM watch_history GROUP BY user_id) WHERE r <= 2
schema: CREATE TABLE watch_history (user_id INTEGER, watch_time INTEGER);
